coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronic acid dressing of hydrophobic carbon nanodots: A self-assembling strategy of hybrid nanocomposites with theranostic potential
coredata.dc:creator: Mauro, Nicolò
Utzeri, Mara Andrea
Drago, Salvatore Emanuele
Nicosia, Aldo
Costa, Salvatore
Cavallaro, Gennara
Giammona, Gaetano
coredata.dc:description:
We propose a rational design of hyaluronic acid-dressed red-emissive carbon dots (CDs), with a well-structured hydrophobic core capable of locally delivering high amount doxorubicin (Doxo) (> 9% w/w) and heat (hyperthermia) in a light stimuli sensitive fashion. We combined in a unique micelle-like superstructure the peculiar optical properties of CDs (NIR photothermal conversion and red fluorescence) with the ability of hyaluronic acid (HA) shell of stabilizing nanomedicines in aqueous environment and recognizing cancer cells overexpressing CD44 receptors on their membranes, thus giving rise to smart theranostic agents useful in cancer imaging and NIR-triggered chemo-phototherapy of solid tumors. Hydrophobic CDs, named HCDs, were used as functional beads to self-assemble amphiphilic HA derivatives carrying polylactic acid side chains (HA-g-PLA), yielding to light-sensitive and biodegradable core-shell superstructures. We explored the biocompatibility and synergistic effects of chemo-phototherapy combination, together with fluorescence imaging, showing the huge potential of the proposed engineering strategy in improving efficacy.

Absorption spectra of CDs and HCDs in DMF (a). Emission spectra of CDs and HCDs in DMF (b): λex = 540 nm. NIR-induced photothermic effect of CDs and HCDs under irradiation at 5 W cm−2: ultrapure water is reported as control (c). Stability of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs in PBS pH 7.4 and acetate buffer pH 5.5 (d). Cumulative drug release kinetic for HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo sample in PBS pH 7.4 and 5.5 compared to the diffusion profile of free Doxo and the NIR-triggered drug release (e). Photothermic effect of HA-g-PLA/HCDs in PBS pH 7.4 at the same concentration tested in drug release study. PBS pH 7.4 was used as control (f).

Self-assembling of the HA-g-PLA copolymer and HCDs into HA-dressed nanocomposite core-shell superstructures (a). Atomic force microscopy of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo (b-b’): height profiles of four selected nanoparticles from Z1 to Z4 (b”).
Cell viability on MCF-7 (a,b) and MDA-MB-231 (a’,b’) after 24 of incubation with HA-g-PLA/HCDs (a, a’) and HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo (b, b’) compared to free Doxo with and without NIR laser exposure.

Cell death pathway focused gene expression analyses in TNBC cells. (a) Heat map representation of the mean-cantered data of RT-qPCR results showing the mRNA levels of analysed genes in presence of HA-g-PLA/HCDs or HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo or Doxo with respect to GAPDH and ACTB with or without photothermal insults (NIR). Gene expression values are coloured from red (low) to green (high). Fold-changes in log (2)-transformed values are represented. (b) Bar plot of the pathway focused gene expression profiling in response to treatments. Bars represent mean ± SD; values were considered statistically significant at p ≤ 0.05.

head.ce:title: Hyaluronic acid dressing of hydrophobic carbon nanodots: A self-assembling strategy of hybrid nanocomposites with theranostic potential

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a rational design of hyaluronic acid-dressed red-emissive carbon dots (CDs), with a well-structured hydrophobic core capable of locally delivering high amount doxorubicin (Doxo) (> 9% w/w) and heat (hyperthermia) in a light stimuli sensitive fashion. We combined in a unique micelle-like superstructure the peculiar optical properties of CDs (NIR photothermal conversion and red fluorescence) with the ability of hyaluronic acid (HA) shell of stabilizing nanomedicines in aqueous environment and recognizing cancer cells overexpressing CD44 receptors on their membranes, thus giving rise to smart theranostic agents useful in cancer imaging and NIR-triggered chemo-phototherapy of solid tumors. Hydrophobic CDs, named HCDs, were used as functional beads to self-assemble amphiphilic HA derivatives carrying polylactic acid side chains (HA-g-PLA), yielding to light-sensitive and biodegradable core-shell superstructures. We explored the biocompatibility and synergistic effects of chemo-phototherapy combination, together with fluorescence imaging, showing the huge potential of the proposed engineering strategy in improving efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The progress in the nanomaterial engineering has led to the development of multifunctional nanosized platforms simultaneously enabling tumor cell recognition, high resolution imaging, local release of heat (hyperthermia) and stimuli-responsive drug delivery as well (Alshehri et al., 2021). This unique combination of features offers the possibility of concurrent monitoring and treatment of tumors (theranostics), improving early diagnosis, therapeutic outcomes, and avoiding limits of unspecific conventional chemotherapy (e.g. serious side effects, low bioavailability, multidrug resistance-MDR) (Kim et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018).

In recent decades, due to its non-invasiveness and high spatial/temporal control, various light-responsive nanosystems were developed for photothermal therapy (PTT) of tumors and controlled drug release (Hak et al., 2021). PTT agents are designed to provoke local temperature increase over 43 °C, by conversion of absorbed near infrared-(NIR) light energy into heat, empowering nanomaterials to selectively activate cell death through the concurrent activation of regulated cell death (RCD) pathways such as necroptosis, autophagy and apoptosis (Nicosia et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018). Besides, scientific evidences on the ability of PTT approach to induce the preferential activation of necroptosis pathway in cancer cells have received considerable attention as novel promising strategy suitable to overcome apoptosis resistance in cancer cells (Sharifi et al., 2019). In particular it seems that the combination of chemo- and phototherapy implies the activation of different RCD routes providing higher anticancer (del Rosal et al., 2018; Nicosia et al., 2020).

Among PTT agents, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising smart drug delivery, NIR photothermal and imaging agents, due to their distinctive tunable multicolour emissions, excellent photostability, high surface/volume ratio and water solubility. Although noble metal-based nanomaterials also shown strong absorption in the NIR region, high photothermal conversion efficiency and good imaging properties, chronic accumulation due to their non-degradable nature limits their medical applications (Craparo et al., 2015; Geng et al., 2018; H. Li et al., 2019). By the way, CDs are very interesting for bioimaging and multifunctional cancer therapy approaches since they are highly biocompatible and can be easily eliminated by renal clearance due to their small size (1–5 nm). Hence, many efforts have been focused on the preparation of CDs endowed with excellent optical performance, exploring various reagents, solvents and synthetic strategies (L. Li & Dong, 2018; Nicolò Mauro, Utzeri, Buscarino, et al., 2020; Wang & Qiu, 2016; Zuo et al., 2016). In particular, the versatility of bottom-up routes and the strategy of doping CDs core with heteroatoms like nitrogen have gained large interest in the scientific community (Crista et al., 2020). Indeed, a wide variety of researches reported in literature demonstrate that N-doped crystalline carbon nanodots offer performing optical properties with good quantum yield (QY), that make them particularly interesting for application in imaging guided chemo-photothermal therapy (A. Sciortino et al., n.d.; Messina et al., 2016; A. Sciortino, Cannizzo, et al., 2018). Furthermore, high surface/volume ratio and rich content of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups make them an excellent nanoplatform for the delivery of hydrophilic anticancer drugs (Scialabba et al., 2019). However, most chemotherapeutic drugs widely used in cancer therapy have hydrophobic nature (Some et al., 2014) (e.g. paclitaxel and Doxo) and therefore, they are difficult to load and delivery by hydrophilic carbon nanodots.

In the light of this consideration, in order to confer hydrophobic surface the appropriate engineering of N-doped crystalline carbon nanodots could be an important strategy to combine their performant theranostic properties with the possibility of delivering elective drugs. On the other hand, the use of hydrophobic CDs in biomedical fields is precluded due to poor solubility in water, which reduces its biodistribution in vivo (Fahmi et al., 2020). Thus, a feasible strategy includes the encapsulation of them into stable core-shell nanosystems in physiological environment in order to dress hydrophobic CDs with hydrophilic macromolecules characterized by optimal biocompatibility and biodistribution profile (Lu et al., 2017). This represents an intelligent strategy as it allows increasing the circulation time in the bloodstream of CDs and, therefore, their accumulation in the tumor tissue (Wong et al., n.d.).

Among the plethora of materials useful for the production of drug delivery systems (DDS), hyaluronic acid (HA), the major constituent of the extracellular matrix (ECM) consisting of a disaccharide repeating unit, assumes great importance to obtain DDS with superior water stability and cell recognition capabilities (Bolhassani et al., 2014; Choi et al., 2019). Indeed, HA is attractive in anticancer therapy owing to its high specificity and affinity for CD44 receptor overexpressed in different cancer cells, affording to receptor-mediated endocytosis (Huang et al., 2019). Furthermore, HA is susceptible to hydrolysis by hyaluronidase (HYAL-1 and HYAL-2) overexpressed in breast and prostate cancer as well (Hill et al., 2015). As consequence, HA-based nanosystems may undergo intracellular disassembly giving rise to ultrasmall by-products susceptible to bioelimination by macrophages or renal clearance (Amorim et al., 2021; X. Chen et al., 2016). However, HA alone do not spontaneously self-assemble into core-shell nanostructure for the delivery of hydrophobic drugs and it requires partial hydrophobization. For instance, many side hydrophobic moieties have been employed to obtain amphiphilic copolymers with self-assemble behavior in aqueous environment such as polyesters or discrete aliphatic chains (Huang et al., 2019). Among these polylactic acid (PLA), poly-ε-caprolactone (PCL) and poly-D,l-lactide-co-glycolide (PLGA) represent a good choice due to their physico-chemical characteristics such as biodegradability and biocompatibility (Su et al., 2019). Besides, biodegradation of self-assembled polyester cores, namely a collapsed composed matrix consisting of hydrophobic drugs and polymer chains, would allow controlled release of actives (Mauro et al., 2019; N. Mauro, Utzeri, Drago, et al., 2020).

Taking this in mind, the aim of this work is the development of a hyaluronic acid copolymer grafted with PLA chains (HA-g-PLA) so as to obtain self-assembled micelle-like superstructures which contain hydrophobic CDs inside the PLA core. These superstructures would serve as NIR-sensitive multifunctional nanocomposites for the on demand image-guided delivery of Doxo and photothermal therapy of tumors. To fulfil this goal, homogeneous crystalline carbon nanodots with excellent red emission and NIR photothermal conversion were prepared, and functionalized at the surface with octylamine (C8) in order to confer proper hydrophobicity. Thus HA-dressed hydrophobic CDs (HCDs) were obtained by one-shot self-assembling of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs mixture. Doxo also was entrapped during the self-assembling process to prove NIR-induced chemo-photothermal effect on breast cancer models such as MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cell lines. The gene expression analysis of the main RCD pathways is investigated as a minimum experimental set to study both biocompatibility and the anticancer effect at molecular level. The aim of this work is to relate on this.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Urea (99%), citric acid (99.5%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol absolute, Sephadex G15 and G25, dialysis tubing MWCO 2 and 14 kDa, Poly(D,l-lactide) acid terminated (PLA; Mw 13,000), Doxo hydrochloride, diethylamine (DEA, 99.5%), trimethylamine (TEA; 99.5%) dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N′-dicicloesilcarbodiimmide (DCC; 99%), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS; 99.5%) phosphate buffer saline (PBS), Triethylamine and Octylamine, were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milan, Italy).

Hyaluronic acid (HA) having a low weight-average molecular weight was prepared by acidic degradation (Shu et al., 2002) of biotechnological HA sodium salt, MW 1500 kDa that has been a generous gift from Novagenit s.r.l. (Italy).

The Molecular weight distribution of the HA and its derivative were determined by size exclusion chromatography (S) apparatus equipped with a pump system, a Universal column (particle size 5 μm) and a 410-differential refractometer (DRI) as a concentration detector, all from Waters (USA). The molecular weight was evaluated by using HA standards (range 100–800 kDa) from Hyalose (USA), 200 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.5)/MeOH 90:10 (v/v) as a mobile phase, 36 ± 0.1 °C, flow rate 0.6 mL min−1 and resulted to be 221 kDa (Mw/Mn = 1.85).
1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker Avance II 300 spectrometer operating at 300.12 MHz.

Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), l-glutamine, penicillin, streptomycin and amphotericin B were purchased from EuroClone (Milan, Italy). CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS) was purchased from Promega (Milan, Italy).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hyaluronic acid grafted poly(D,l-lactide) copolymer (HA-g-PLA)
ce:section.ce:para:
The N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) derivative of PLA, namely PLA-NHS, was obtained as previously reported (Palumbo et al., 2006). Separately, a tetrabultylamonium (TBA, 1 eq.) salified HA (
M ¯
n = 112 kDa, PD = 1.64), named HA-TBA (600 mg), was solubilized in DMSO (48 mL) and DEA (576 μL) was added as a catalyst. Then, PLA-NHS (50 mg) was dissolved in anhydrous DMSO (6 mL) and added to the HA-TBA solution dropwise in about 1 h with stirring. The reaction was kept under argon atmosphere at 40 °C for 48 h, and, subsequently, the TBA was removed by ion exchanging with sodium cations by fluxing the organic solution in a cationic exchange resin (Dowex 50 W × 8–200), and the solution was precipitated in acetone and washed up in the same solvent five times. Finally, HA-g-PLA was dispersed in water and dialysed against a sodium chloride aqueous solution (5% w/v) for 3 days and against water for 2 days by using dialysis tubing with a molecular-weight-cut-off (MWCO) of 14,000 Da. The product was recovered as a white powder after freeze-drying. Yield: 71%.
1H NMR (300 MHz, D2O/THF, 25 °C): δ 1.57 and δ 1.68 (2d, 3HPLA, –O–CO–CH(CH3)–O– of PLA), δ 2.12 (s, 3HHA, –NH–CO–CH3), δ 5.40 ppm (m, 1HPLA –O–CO–CH(CH3)– of PLA).

SEC:
M ¯
n = 168 kDa:
M ¯
w = 225 kDa; PD = 1.34.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the critical aggregation concentration (CAC) of HA-g-PLA
ce:section.ce:para: The critical aggregation concentration was estimated spectrofluorimetrically by employing pyrene as a hydrophobic fluorescent probe. Briefly, 5 μL of pyrene solution in acetone (6 × 10−5 M) was deposited in a tube test and subsequently, after complete evaporation of acetone, 500 μL of water dispersion of HA-g-PLA copolymers with a concentration within 9 × 10−4 - 0.25 mg mL−1 was added and left one night before the analysis. Then, the emission spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 333 nm. The I1/I3 ratio of the intensities of the first and the third peak of fluorescence spectrum of pyrene was used to detect the formation of polymeric micelles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared under solvothermal condition (160 °C, 4 h) from urea (6 g) and citric acid (3 g) in anhydrous DMF (30 mL) as reported elsewhere (Scialabba et al., 2019). The product was precipitated in ethanol, the precipitate collected by centrifuging (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and redispersed in ultrapure water by sonicating (15 min × 3). The heterogeneous aqueous dispersion of CDs was purified by size exclusion chromatography (S) combining three types of Sephadex with increasing MWCO (G10 ➔ G25). Subsequently, only fractions of CDs with homogeneous dimensions, good red fluorescence and NIR-sensitive photothermal property were selected. Yield: 15%. Hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) were synthesized solubilizing CDs (20 mg) in DMF (5 mL) and adding octylamine (C8) (26 μL, 0.157 mmol), N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) (32.38 mg, 0.157 mmol) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (18.07 mg, 0.157 mmol) at once under vigorous stirring. The reaction was kept under stirring (40 rpm) at 25 °C for 24 h. The product was then filtered and purified by dialysis (1 kDa) against water pH 5 for 3 days. Finally, the product was retrieved by centrifuging at 13,000 rpm for 15 min. The pellet was freeze-dried to get a dark powder of HCDs.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker FAST-SCAN) was performed to evaluate size distribution of CDs in soft tapping mode. Samples (0.1 mg L−1, 10 μL) were deposited on a mica substrate and dried in vacuum (10 mbar). We used triangular FastScan A probes (resonance frequency = 1400 kHz, tip radius = 5 nm). Size distribution was attained by the heights of CDs obtained by AFM data.

The structure of CDs was characterized using a JEOL JEMS-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operating at 200 kV electron energy. Each sample was prepared in ultrapure water (1 mg L−1) and deposited on a 400 μm mesh Cu grid covered by a holey amorphous carbon film, with a nominal thickness of 3 nm.

Surface functional groups of both CDs and HCDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Bruker Alpha II spectrometer) in KBr pellets. Spectra where recorded within the range 400–4000 cm−1 (scan times: 24; resolution: 4 cm−1). Additionally, the surface of HCDs was studied by 1H NMR to evaluate the degree of surface functionalization with C8 moieties using an exact amount of acetone (5 μL) as internal standard. Samples were dissolved in DMF-d6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization and photothermal study of HCDs and parent compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption properties of CDs and HCDs in DMF were carried out by a double beam spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2401PC) in the range of 200–800 nm.
The emission spectra were recorded using a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC) under excitation wavelength of 540 nm and a resolution of 2 nm.

The photothermal properties of CDs (≈4.289×1014 nanoparticles) and HCDs (≈3.002×1014 nanoparticles) were investigated by irradiating aqueous dispersions (0.1 mg mL−1, 0.2 mL) with a 810 nm diode laser (GBox 15A/B by GIGA Laser) with power of 5 W cm−2 up to 300 s. The hyperthermia kinetics were recorded monitoring the temperature at scheduled time intervals by using a fiber optic (CEM®).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembling of hyaluronic acid-dressed HCDs (HA-g-PLA/HCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the hyaluronic acid-dressed hydrophobic carbon nanodots, HCDs were dispersed in DMSO (2 mg mL−1, 2 mL), and added to an aqueous dispersion of HA-g-PLA (2 mg mL−1, 10 mL) dropwise. The mixture was left under stirring at 4 °C overnight, then purified by exhaustive dialysis (12–14 kDa) and retrieved as sponge powder after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution was evaluated by DLS measurements using a Malvern Zetasizer NanoZS instrument equipped with a 632 nm laser with a fixed scattering angle of 173°. The analysis were performed on 1 mL of HA-g-PLA/HCDs dispersion in PBS pH 7.4 (0.2 mg mL−1). Z-average and PDI were obtained from the analysis of correlograms.

Zeta-potential analysis were performed by aqueous electrophoresis measurements at 25 °C, using the same apparatus for the DLS measurements, on HA-g-PLA/HCDs dispersion in 0.01 mM KCl (0.2 mg mL−1). The Zeta-potential values (mV) were calculated from electrophoretic mobility using the Smoluchowski relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs stability in aqueous dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The HA-g-PLA/HCDs stability under different conditions mimicking physiological (pH 7.4) and tumor microenvironment media (pH 5.5) was evaluated in PBS pH 7.4 or acetate buffer pH 5.5 at 37 °C up to 72 h of incubation. The size distribution of samples was measured at established time intervals by DLS as above described and reported as a function of the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Surface wetting was evaluated by contact angle measurements carried out using a DSA25 by Kruss Germany. In order to assess the equilibrium time, contact angle was measured after the deposition of the water drop as well as after 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembling of Doxo-loaded hyaluronic acid-dressed HCDs (HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo)
ce:section.ce:para: The HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo were prepared as above described for the empty micelle-like superstructures, but adding Doxo (6.25 mg) and TEA (7.5 μL) to the dispersion of HCDs in DMSO before self-assembling. Furthermore, the powder was redispersed in ultrapure water (1.5 mL) and the free drug eventually present was removed by using Sephadex G-25 as stationary phase and ultrapure water as eluent. A reddish powder was obtained after freeze-drying. Yield: 67%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug-loading and drug release kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of Doxo loaded into HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo was calculated spectrophotometrically measuring the absorbance at 481 nm and comparing data with those of a calibration curve obtained with free Doxo standards (R2 = 0.998; 0.1–0.025 mg mL−1). Samples were prepared by dispersing a known amount of HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo in DMSO/H2O/0.1 N HCl (1:0.9:0.1) by sonication before the reading. The same amount of empty micelles were used to subtract the absorbance owing to scattering phenomena.

For the drug release kinetic experiments, either an amount of HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo corresponding to a drug concentration of 0.115 mg mL−1 or free Doxo were dispersed either in PBS pH 7.4 (2.5 mL) or acetate buffer pH 5.5 (2.5 mL) and placed into a dialysis tubing of 2 kDa MWCO against 20 mL of the same medium. At scheduled time intervals, 0.5 mL of the external medium was withdrawn and replaced with equal volume of fresh medium. The cumulative drug release over time was obtained by calculating the amount of Doxo released spectrophotometrically using the same procedure above reported.

The photothermal-triggered drug release kinetics were evaluated by irradiating the dispersion of HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo at pH 7.4 or 5.5 (eq. Doxo = 0.115 mg mL−1) before the dialysis process, using a NIR laser with a power of 5 W cm−2 for 300 s. The heating kinetic was previously evaluated at the same condition used in the drug release study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological characterization
Cytocompatibility assay of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytocompatibility of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs was evaluated by MTS assay on two different human breast cancer cell lines (MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7). Cells were incubated in a 96 wells plate at a density of 1.5×104 cells/well (200 μL) and grown in supplemented Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM). After 24 h, the medium was replaced with a dispersion of HA-g-PLA/HCDs in DMEM (200 μL) in the concentration range of 0.271–0.00054 mg mL−1. After 24 and 48 h, samples were removed from each well and 100 μL of fresh DMEM and 20 μL of MTS assay solution was added after washing cells twice with DPBS pH 7.4. Then, cells were incubated for additional 2 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 before reading the absorbance at 492 nm using a Microplate Reader (Multiskan Ex, Thermo Labsystems, Finland). Cell viability was expressed as percentage of living cells with respect to the untreated control of seeded cells (taken as 100%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer activity of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo with and without NIR exposure
ce:section.ce:para: The anticancer activity of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo was assessed by MTS assay as above described and using different concentrations of micelles corresponding to equivalent concentration of free Doxo ranging from 50 to 0.1 μM. The anticancer effect was reported as cell viability reduction of two breast cancer cell lines, namely MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7, against untreated control cells, after 24 and 48 h of treatment.

In another experimental set, the effect of the combination of NIR-triggered hyperthermia and Doxo local release was investigated exposing both cancer cell lines incubated with increasing amount of HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo to a 810 nm diode laser with a power of 7 W cm−2 for 300 s. In particular, cells were seeded in a 96 wells plate with a density of 1.5×104 cells/well (200 μL) and grown for 24 h in DMEM. Successively, the medium was replaced with a dispersion of either HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo (50 to 0.1 μM) or equivalent amount of the bare HA-g-PLA/HCDs (0.271–0.00054 mg mL−1) and incubated for 24 or 48 h. After this time, each well was irradiated up to 300 s, wells were washed with DPBS pH 7.4 twice, and MTS assay was performed as described above. The temperature increment as function of the laser exposure time was previously evaluated at the intermediate concentration tested in vitro (30 μg mL−1). The anticancer activity of the compound was compared with those of free Doxo at the same concentrations considered, treated and untreated with NIR laser. All experimental biological set was performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction and first-strand cDNA synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: For the evaluation of gene expression, cells were incubated in a 96 wells plate at a density of 104 cells/well and after 24 h, medium was replaced with a dispersion of either HA-g-PLA/HCDs, HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo or free Doxo in DMEM (200 μL), at a concentration suitable to reduce cell viability of 50% (IC50). After 24 h, each well was irradiated with a diode laser with power density of 7 W cm−2 for 300 s. In parallel, the same experiment was conducted without laser irradiation.

Total RNA was purified from control and treated cells using Trizol Reagent (Invitrogen Corporation, Carlsbad, USA) and following the manufacturer's instructions. RNA concentrations and purity (Abs260, Abs260/280, and Abs260/230) were spectrophotometrically verified using Eppendorf 6131 BioPhotometer (Eppendorf AG, Hamburg, Germany). RNA integrity was checked on 1.5% agarose denaturing gel and RNAs were stored at −80 °C for future use. Purified RNA was treated with Deoxyribonuclease I, Amplification Grade (Sigma-Aldrich) to remove any residual genomic DNA contamination, and DNAseI was inactivated by adding 50 mM EDTA. First-strand cDNA was synthesized from DNAseI-treated RNA samples (about 100 ng) using the High-Capacity cDNA Reverse Transcription Kit (ThermoFischer Scientific) following the manufacturer's instructions. All the cDNAs were tested by PCR using GAPDH, and ACTB primers and were diluted 1:10 prior to use in Real-Time qPCR experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene expression by real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR)
ce:section.ce:para: The qPCRs were performed using the BIO-RAD CFX96 system with BrightGreen 5× qPCR MasterMix (Applied Biological Materials Inc., Richmond, Canada) as detection chemistry. The ACTB and GAPDH were selected as control genes because of their expression stability in all the tested conditions. A normalization factor was calculated based on geometric averaging of the expression level of these reference genes and it was used to quantify the expression levels of the target genes (Nicosia et al., 2020). Primer sequences used in this study are listed as supplementary materials in Table S1. Quantitative real-time PCRs were conducted according to the manufacturer's recommended procedures, and each reaction was repeated in triplicate. The amplification conditions were the following: initial denaturation at 95 °C for 10 min and 40 cycles of 95 °C for 30 s and 60 °C for 50 s, followed by a melting curve from 60 to 95 °C. Amplicons were detected by agarose gel analysis after each PCR to confirm the amplification of the specific gene. Experiments were performed in triplicate. Gene expression results presented as heat maps were generated via Heatmapper (available at http://heatmapper.ca/expression/). The mRNA levels are represented as mean centered. Gene expression results reported in bar charts and expressed as a mean value ± SD. Significant differences between values of different treated groups and the reference control groups were determined by t-test using Statistica 6.0 (StatSoft, Tulsa, OK, USA). The p values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake study of HA-g-PLA/HCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of HA-g-PLA/HCDs of entering cancer cells was evaluated on MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and 16-HBE using an Axio Cam MRm (Zeiss) fluorescence microscope. Cells were seeded in a 8 wells plate with a density of 1×104 cells/well (200 μL) and grown in DMEM for 24 h. After that, the medium was replaced with a dispersion of the sample in fresh culture medium (25 μg mL−1) and incubated for 4 h and 24 h. Successively, the dispersion was removed, cells were washed twice with DPBS pH 7.4 and nuclei were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) adding 100 μL of DAPI solution and incubating for 5 min. For the MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cell lines, internalization studies were repeated pre-incubating them with a HA solution (150 μL, 10 μg mL−1) for 30 min, and then adding 125 μL of HA-g-PLA/CDs(50 μg mL−1). After 4 h and 24 h the medium was removed, cells whased with fresh PBS pH 7.4 (200 μL) and nuclei stained adding 10 μL of DAPI solution for 5 min. Before acquiring the uptake images, each well was washed with DPBS pH 7.4 three times. Cells self-fluorescence was removed by setting up the microscope using untreated cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
Synthesis of the amphiphilic hyaluronic acid derivative (HA-g-PLA)
ce:section.ce:para:
An amphiphilic copolymer of HA named HA-g-PLA, which consists of HA (
M ¯
n = 112 kDa) grafted with low molecular weight PLA chains (
M ¯
n = 13 kDa), was synthetized by esterification between an end-chain activated N-hydroxysuccinimidyl ester of PLA and hydroxyl groups of HA under mild basic conditions (Scheme 1 ). The molecular weight of the main HA backbone was chosen to avoid reaching the typical gelling point of high molecular weight HA, which would have had adverse effects on assembling mechanisms, but still maintaining HA biological properties up to 10 mg mL−1 (Pravata et al., 2008). While, PLA chains were selected to provide discrete hydrophobization of the HA backbone so as to provoke self-aggregation in aqueous environment. The reaction was carried out using a PLA/HA molar ratio of 0.1 in order to partially functionalize the starting HA chains with hydrophobic side chains, which are able to induce suitable aggregation into micelles in aqueous environment. The derivatization degree of the HA, obtained by 1H NMR comparing the integrals of protons of HA units with those of the PLA side chains, was about 2% on a molar basis, indicating the presence of about four PLA moieties per HA chain (Fig. S1).
According to 1H NMR data, the SEC analysis reveals a copolymer with an average number molecular weight of 168 kDa.

The ability of HA-g-PLA to self-assemble into stable core-shell nanostructures was preliminary studied by the pyrene fluorescence assay (Fig. S2). It is noteworthy that HA-g-PLA is able to aggregate into nanostructures with hydrophobic core at a concentration higher than 43 μg mL−1. This amphiphilic copolymer of HA was selected to provoke the spontaneous self-assembling of core-shell supramolecular systems bearing hydrophobic carbon nanodots inside the core so as to entrap high amount of anticancer drugs such as Doxo. Besides, the hydrophilic HA shell was chosen to impart active targeting features toward cancer cells given the affinity of HA for the CD44 receptor overexpressed in several cancer cells (Fiorica et al., n.d.; Huang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and physicochemical characterization of the hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Homogeneous crystalline N-doped carbon nanodots endowed with a sharp red emission band with quantum yield (QY) of 6% were synthetized by a bottom-up method reported elsewhere (N. Mauro, Utzeri, Drago, et al., 2020; Nicosia et al., 2020; Scialabba et al., 2019). These CDs have homogeneous size distribution (≈ 4.4 ± 0.8 nm) as calculated from the heights observed in the AFM analysis (Fig. 1 a-a’). Besides, as showed by HRTEM analyses reported in Fig. 1 b-b’, they exhibit the typical graphitic d-spacing observed for β-C3N4 carbon nanodots (Messina et al., 2016; A. Sciortino, Gazzetto, et al., 2018).

The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ), that reveals strong carbonyl bands at about 1710 cm−1 implying the presence of many carboxyl groups at the CDs surface. These functional groups, apart from the possibility of serving as reactive groups for further surface functionalization, make the CDs surface highly hydrophilic and unable to bind anionic drug or hydrophobic drugs such as most anticancer drugs (Some et al., 2014). Besides, electrostatic repulsions between the CDs surface and the carboxyl groups of HA-g-PLA impinge on the formation of supramolecular assembled nanosystems able to deliver drugs in a photothermal-sensitive way.

For these reasons, in this work we propose hydrophobization of CDs with discrete aliphatic chains such as octylamine (C8) and surface passivation of the carboxyl groups via amide coupling as an incisive strategy to provoke spontaneous self-assembling of the amphiphilic copolymer HA-g-PLA in the presence of hydrophobic CDs, henceforth named HCDs, thus leading to supramolecular aggregates with core-shell micellar-like structure (HA-g-PLA/HCDs). These HA-g-PLA/HCDs combine the typical optical properties of CDs (i.e., fluorescence imaging and good photothermal conversion) with the delivery features of amphiphilic HA derivatives toward hydrophobic anticancer drugs, which is highly desired in theranostics chemo-photothermal cancer therapy. Thus red-emitting CDs were conjugated with octylamine by amide coupling reaction between carboxyl functions of the bare CDs surface and the amine function of octylamine (Scheme 2 ). The activation of carboxyl groups occurred by means of N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) coupling giving rise to, after purification, carbon dots bearing hydrophobic aliphatic chains at the surface (HCDs). The adopted synthetic scheme is scalable and reproducible in term of functionalization degree with high yield (60%), and allowed obtaining an efficient surface functionalization under mild conditions.

To evaluate how surface functionalization with C8 moieties changed the affinity of CDs for hydrophobic compounds surface wettability was investigated by equilibrium contact angle measurements (Fig. 2 a-a’). As expected HCDs displays a marked hydrophobicity in comparison with the bare CDs (θ > 90° and θ < 90° for the HCDs and CDs, respectively). The feasibility of the surface passivation with hydrophobic agents was also confirmed by the observation of different partition of bare CDs and HCDs in a o/w biphasic system (CHCl3/H2O). As shown in Fig. 2b, surface functionalization of CDs with C8 chains induced significant changes in the partition behavior for HCDs, resulting much more affine to the CHCl3 phase. By contrast, bare CDs were distributed in water. Once can image that the high affinity of HCDs for the organic phase allows HCDs incorporation inside the hydrophobic core of supramolecular systems obtained by self-assembling of amphiphilic copolymers such as HA-g-PLA.

Surface functionalization of CDs was also evaluated by FTIR analysis, confirming the presence of amide bonds and aliphatic chains, accompanied with the reduction of surface carboxyl groups (A. Sciortino, Gazzetto, et al., 2018). As show in Fig. 2c, the bare CDs show OH (3420 cm−1) and carbonyl stretching vibrations (1708 cm−1) that indicate the presence of several COOH reactive groups suitable to amide coupling. Both the reduction of the COOH band at 3327 cm−1 and the appearance of the I amide band at 1630 cm−1 in the HCDs spectrum suggest that the functionalization of the bare CDs successfully occurred. Furthermore, the appearance of aliphatic bands, attributable to CH vibrations (2927–2852 cm−1), confirmed the presence of alkyl chains on the HCDs surface.

The amount of C8 chains on the HCDs surface was evaluated by 1H NMR using acetone as internal standard. Fig. S3 shows the characteristic peaks of octylamine at 0.89 ppm and at 1.28 ppm, relative to the CH3 and to CH2 respectively. The depletion of the characteristic peak at 2.58 ppm, related to the CH2 bound to the amine, and the shift of this peak to higher fields suggest a successful conjugation via amide linkage.

The functionalization degree was calculated comparing the peak relative to acetone (6H; δ 2.13 ppm) with that relative to octylamine (3H; δ 0.89 ppm), according to the following equation:
n A

where n
A and n
C are respectively the moles of acetone and octylamine in the analysed sample.

The functionalization degree in octylamine was found to be 30 ± 2% w/w, which correspond to a functionalization of 3.8 meq g−1 of CDs. Considering that from previous titration data it is known that the bare CDs are characterized by an amount of carboxyl groups equal to 5.5 meq. g−1, under the used experimental conditions the functionalization in octylamine proceeds for 69% of the available carboxyl groups(Scialabba et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of the hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: With the aim of understanding if HCDs can be used in IG-PPT applications, their photothermal and red fluorescence properties were evaluated and compared with the bare CDs. As showed in Fig. 3a HCDs displayed a complex absorption spectrum characterized by different bands in the entire UV/UVvis range, similar to the bare CDs spectrum.

The emission spectrum of HCDs and bare CDs were collected in DMF upon excitation at 540 nm. As reported in Fig. 3b, the emission spectrum of HCDs was comparable to that of bare CDs. Indeed, although HCDs showed a slightly reduced photoluminescence compared to bare CDs, no quenching was observed, since HCDs preserve the emission profile of the starting CDs. Unlike other CDs with inherent hydrophobic surface, obtained from solvothermal decomposition processes using hydrophobic compounds such as melamine and benzoic acid, the post-functionalization of the CDs surface with C8 moieties do not suffer from π-π stacking interactions or aggregation phenomena which eventually induce the turning off of the characteristic emission bands of CDs (Yang et al., 2019). This confirms that core structure and surface passivation have a decisive and influent role on fluorescence emission phenomena (A. Sciortino et al., n.d.). Here, the emission spectrum was characterized by a main peak at about 570 nm with two shoulders at about 608 and 645 nm. The photoluminescence in the biological transparent window (red-NIR range) displayed by HCDs is of particular interest and necessary for high resolution fluorescence imaging, useful in cancer diagnosis of primary and secondary (Nicolò Mauro, Utzeri, Buscarino, et al., 2020; Smith et al., 2009).

The ability of HCDs to act as photothermal agent in IG-PTT converting 810 nm NIR light into heat was evaluated irradiating a water dispersion of either bare CDs or HCDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and measuring the temperature increase (∆T) as a function of the laser exposure. In order to compare the photothermic effect each other, temperature increases were normalized by the number of dots at equal concentration. It might be noticed that HCDs possess a remarkable photothermal conversion capacity, greater than that shown by bare CDs (Fig. 3c). HCDs reach the minimum temperature of hyperthermia (43 °C), needed to eradicate tumors, after about 42 s of laser exposure, whereas for the bare CDs a laser exposure four times higher is required to reach the same temperature. The higher photothermal conversion observed for the HCDs in aqueous environment can be ascribed to hydrophobic interactions at the surface, which lead aggregation of the HCDs and restriction of the surfaces' intramolecular rotation around C8 bonds. Hence, only vibrations can contribute to dissipation phenomena giving rise heating. On the whole, the red emission properties displayed by the HCDs and the efficient photothermal conversion capability make them a potential candidate for cancer IG-PTT applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembling of the hyaluronic acid-dressed HCDs (HA-g-PLA/HCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained HCDs were employed as functional excipient to obtain photoactivable supramolecular nanocomposites endowed with red fluorescence, NIR photothermic features and able to be internalized through active targeting through the affinity of the hyaluronic acid shell for the CD44 receptor overexpressed in most cancer cells (Huang et al., 2019). The HA-g-PLA/HCDs were prepared by spontaneous self-assembling of the HA-g-PLA copolymer in a mixture of water and DMSO, considering a HCDs theoretical loading of 20% on a weight basis (Fig. 4a). Dialysis of the dispersion against water allowed the diffusion of DMSO and the complete self-assembling of hybrid core-shell HA-g-PLA/HCDs superstructures, without hinting at precipitation of HCDs. Besides, the diffusion of free HCDs outside the membrane was completely excluded by the absence of the typical absorption bands in the waste. This indirectly suggests their complete encapsulation within the hydrophobic PLA core of HA-g-PLA micelle-like superstructures.

The encapsulation of HCDs inside the PLA core was also confirmed by Zeta-potential analysis, where no significant changes in the surface charge distribution occurred after the incorporation of HCDs in the assembled copolymer (≈ −33 mV and ≈ −26 mV, respectively), thus suggesting that HCDs are distributed only throughout the hydrophobic core (Table 1 ). The reason can be attributed to the higher partition coefficient and to the lower carboxyl functions density at the surface observed for the HCDs in comparison to the bare CDs. Indeed, the incorporation of the bare CDs under the same condition was not allowed both owing to electrostatic repulsion with the anionic charges of the HA shell together with the their very low partition coefficient which limits hydrophobic interactions.

The strong chain entanglements between the C8 moieties of HCDs and the PLA side chains of the HA-g-PLA significantly impinge on the self-assembling of the superstructures. In particular, the encapsulation of HCDs within the PLA core induces a sharp reduction of the nanosystem diameter (from 739 nm to 330 nm), implying the formation of a collapsed and well-structured hydrophobic core (Table 1). This would provide a suitable reservoir for the controlled and photo-triggered release of hydrophobic drugs such as Doxo or related anticancer compounds.

The physicochemical stability of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs both in physiological medium and in the tumor microenvironment (TME) was carried out in PBS pH 7.4 and acetate buffer pH 5.5, respectively (Fig. 3d). The size distribution obtained by DLS measurements was studied as a function of the incubation time at 37 °C up to 72 from the incubation. HA-g-PLA/HCDs display excellent stability under physiological conditions, since the size distribution remains constant over time (about 330 nm). As the size distribution of HA-g-PLA/HCDs appeared strongly affected by the acidic conditions which mimic TME, undergoing a sharp increase at t0 up to about 700 nm and a gradual decrease over time (550 nm after 72 h at 37 °C). This behavior suggest a rapid cleavage of the ester bonds between the PLA and the HA moieties at the surface, accompanied to simultaneous degradation of the PLA chains under acidic conditions, thus provoking sudden swelling of the HA shell and the consequent surface erosion of the PLA core. This mechanism explain well the biphasic trend observed in Fig. 3d at pH 5.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the drug-loaded hyaluronic acid-dressed HCDs (HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo)
ce:section.ce:para: The Doxo-loaded hyaluronic acid-dressed HCDs, namely HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo, were prepared by self-assembling of the HA copolymer in the presence of both HCDs and Doxo (20% w/w). The idea was to solubilize high amount of Doxo during the formation of the collapsed hydrophobic core of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs in aqueous dispersion. The amount of Doxo loaded into the HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo was measured spectrophotometrically, and was found to be 9.63 ± 0.049% (w/w), corresponding to an encapsulation efficiency of about 48%. This encapsulation efficiency is far higher than other examples reported in the literature for similar self-assembled amphiphilic copolymers (Pitarresi et al., 2010). This result can be reasonably ascribed to the presence of HCDs in the core of the superstructures, which offer a greater hydrophobic surface available for interaction with the drug.

The morphological characterization of these superstructures was attained by atomic force microscopy (AFM), where nanoaggregates of about 60 nm in height were observed in dry state (Fig. 4 b-b”). The size distribution of the dried nanocomposite superstructures is much lower if compared to the same sample in water (about 300 nm, Table 1). Obviously, the lower size distribution observed is ascribed to the lack of the hydration sphere in the dried micelles if compared with the sample in water dispersion. This suggests that tuning the molecular weight of the HA shell would allow controlling the average size of these superstructures as function of the specific application. It might be noticed that neither free HCDs nor free Doxo was observed in the sample, suggesting that the encapsulation of both elements was completely accomplished during the self-assembling procedure adopted.

As shown in Table 1 no significant differences were observed between the drug-loaded and free drug HA-g-PLA/HCDs in terms of Zeta-potential and size distribution, suggesting that Doxo do not perturb chain entanglements mechanisms during self-assembling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermic effect and drug release kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo to release the drug payload in a controlled fashion was established in PBS pH 7.4 (physiological conditions) and acetate buffer pH 5.5 (tumor microenvironment-TME conditions) at 37 °C, with and without a NIR laser exposure at suitable power density. As shown in Fig. 3e HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo offer a slow and sustained drug release profile over time under physiological conditions, avoiding the undesirable burst effect correlated to the use of free drugs. Indeed, the free Doxo freely diffused throughout in 2 h, displaying a sharp burst effect in the first minutes after the incubation at 37 °C. Whereas HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo retained its payload, releasing only 10% of drug payload after 48 h. On the contrary, HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo release the drug payload faster at pH 5.5 (about 58% within 48 h), suggesting that they can favorably release Doxo in the TME in a pH-dependent modality (Fig. 3e). This correlates well with the different behavior of the superstructures observed in the two media, considering the occurrence of hydrolytic phenomena in the polylactic acid moieties at acidic pH, which lead to swelling and consequent erosion of the nanostructure (Fig. 3d) thus offering a larger surface for the diffusion of Doxo.

The NIR-triggered photothermal effect of nano-heaters such as HCDs usually allows a faster drug release due to a thermal-induced destabilization of the hydrophobic core where HCDs are entrapped (Nam et al., 2018; Nicolò Mauro, Scialabba, Agnello, et al., 2020a; Scialabba et al., 2019). Indeed, the NIR-induced drug release kinetic reported in Fig. 3e shows that HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo is able to release an amount of Doxo two times bigger at pH 7.4 if compared to the not irradiated nanosystems. A similar trend, even if much more restrained, was observed at pH 5.5 (66% release vs 58%). The light-triggered Doxo release provides a good chance to locally release the drug payload in the site of action on demand, thus reflecting in the biodistribution of the actives in the body minimizing side effects and avoiding multidrug resistance. The feasibility of on demand Doxo release under hyperthermia (≈ 43 °C) was also established evaluating the NIR-induced temperature increase at the same concentration tested in drug release study and under a 810 nm diode laser exposure for 300 s at 5 W cm−2 (Fig. 3f). As reported in Fig. 3f, HA-g-PLA-HCDs show good photothermal conversion properties, reaching the minimum temperature for hyperthermia (42–43 °C) after less than 200 s of irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological characterization
Cytocompatibility and anticancer efficacy assay
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxic effects of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs and HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo was carried out on cultures of two different breast cancer cell lines, namely MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7, after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 5 ).

Cell viability values of MCF-7 (Fig. 5 a) and MDA-MB-231 (Fig. 5 a’) treated with HA-g-PLA/HCDs were optimal into the entire range of tested concentrations (100%), demonstrating the high cytocompatibility of the nanosystems within the range of concentration tested (up to 300 μg mL−1). Cell viability assay was repeated on both cell lines above considered, applying a 810 nm NIR laser sourse of 7 W cm−2 power density to evaluate the NIR-triggered hyperthermia effects due to the empity superstrucutres. Hyperthermia levels were established on a dispersion of HA-g-PLA-HCDs in DMEM (30 μg mL−1), irradiating the sample with 810 nm diode laser for 300 s with power fitted at 7 W cm−2. Hence, we established the experimental setting to give rise suitable hyperthermia (44–47 °C) during in vitro cell culture conditions. This power density and exposure was proven absolutely biocompatible by other studies published elsewhere using both cell lines (N. Mauro et al., 2017; Nicolò Mauro et al., 2015; Nicolò Mauro, Scialabba, Agnello, et al., 2020b). Fig. 5 a-a’ shows that a cetain dose-pedendent photothermal-induced damage occurred for both cell lines, with a remarked cytotoxic effect at the highest dose (>150 μg mL−1).

For the cytocompatibility study of the drug-loaded HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo nanosystem, equivalent amount of HA-g-PLA/HCDs (i.e., from 0.271 to 0.00054 mg mL−1) were employed allowing the evaluation of synergistic effects owing to Doxo-mediated damages. A dose-dependent cytotoxic effect was observed for both cell lines, as cell viability decreased with the increase of the amount of HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo (Figure b-b’). In order to evaluate the NIR-triggered hyperthermia effect on cancer cells, combined with the on demand chemotherapy due to local drug release, a second set of experiment was performed. After 24 h of incubation with HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo, wells were irradiated with an 810 nm diode laser using the selected hyperthermia condition (300 s, 7 W cm−2 power density). As shown in Fig. 5 b-b’, cell viability exponentially decreased following a dose-dependent response which resemble those observed for the unexposed samples for both cell lines considered, even if with some qualification. In particular, when hyperthermia is combined to on demand chemotherapy owing to Doxo release from HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo, the cytotoxic effect is much more marked if compared with the empity nanosystem, reaching almost 0% in cell viability at concentration of 150 μg mL−1 (eq. Doxo dosage of 20 μM). Besides, the potency of the chemo-photothermal treatment with HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo, expressed as IC50 value (Table S2), was comparable to the one calculated for free Doxo, even if at lower available dose (Doxo release is only 20% after this laser exposure conditions). Indeed, usually cytotoxic effects due to the free drug is higher since it is promptly available and freely diffuse throughout the cell culture provoking damages. This trend can be explained considering synergistic effects of NIR-assisted chemo-photrotherapy provided by HA-g-PLA-HCDs@Doxo. It is noteworthy that MCF-7 are more sensitive than MDA-MB-231 cells (IC50 0.56 μM vs 0.94 μM). Therefore, taking into account the active targeting abilities of the HA shell in HA-g-PLA/HCDs and their potential as fluorescence imaging agent, on demand NIR-triggered drug release and chemo-photothermal cytotoxic effects, these nanocomposite superstructures represent promising anticancer theranostic platforms useful for IG-PTT applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene expression analysis on treated MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: With the aim of confirming HA-g-PLA-HCDs cytocompatibility at the molecular level and testing presumptive cell death mechanisms triggered by HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo micelles upon NIR irradiation, the differential mRNA expression levels of 10 genes (BECN1, CASP7, ATG3, CASP9, BAX, PARP1,BCL-2, RIPK1, BCL-XL, and TNFα), recognised to exert a functional role in cell death pathways (apoptotic, autophagic, and necrotic/necroptotic pathways), were assessed in MDA-MB-231 and MCF7 breast cancer cell lines (Fig. 6 ). Cells were treated using all IC50 values identified in the cell viability assay (Table S2), and for empty HA-g-PLA/HCDs comparable doses were employed. These experimental conditions were chosen so as to avoid dose-dependent effects in response to different chemo-photothermal insults.

A comparison between cells treated with HA-g-PLA/HCDs and unexposed MDA-MB-231 showed a similar transcriptional profile. No significant differences in gene expression were measured in HA-g-PLA/HCDs treated cells, with negligible effects, if any, on the activation of a death response. Overlapping profiles also occurred in NIR-treated cells and NIR exposed MDA-MB-231 after HA-g-PLA/HCDs incubation which remained similar to controls (Fig. 6a and b). This corroborate cell viability data which highlight the high cytocompatibility profile of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs after 24 h of incubation.

As expected, MDA-MB-231 exposure to Doxo provided a different gene expression pattern when compared to the control. Among the gene set analysed, we found a number of genes associated with apoptotic cell death, exhibiting significant differential expression (Fig. 6a). In particular, the expression of proapoptotic genes as TNFα, BAX, and CASP7 is increased, together with a reduction of the gene expression levels of pro-survival factors as BCL2 (Fig. 6b). These results confirm the activation of an apoptotic cell death machinery in response to Doxo occurred at the IC50 values (Minotti et al., 2004; Pilco-Ferreto & Calaf, 2016). It emerges also a concurrent upregulation of BECN1 and BCL-XL. It is known that BECN1 plays as a pivot in the mechanism of autophagosoma formation; additionally, the BH3 domain of Beclin 1 is inhibited by binding to Bcl-XL (Kang et al., 2011; Luo & Rubinsztein, 2010). Therefore the cross-talk between apoptosis and autophagy (Q. Chen et al., 2018), which may transform the commitment toward a specific pathway, could be also supposed to be active in this experimental setting. Considering the mRNA profiles and magnitude of the measured changes, according to cell viability data, similar results were also obtained after NIR exposure of Doxo-treated cells (Fig. 6a and b). This suggests that the laser treatment, as herein carried out, does not exert additional effects to Doxo on MDA-MB-231 viability.

The laser treatment of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo resulted in a transcriptional pattern resembling those above measured. However, it should be noticed that the co-occurrence of Doxo-related DNA ruptures and the NIR treatment appears to reinforce the upregulation of the pro-apoptotic genes BAX, and CASP7, in addition to a huge downregulation of the anti-apoptotic gene BCL2, thus likely triggering the activation of events leading to apoptosis. This implies a synergistic anticancer effect brought about by the combination of chemo- and photothermal-induced traumas.

The transcriptional response of MCF-7 upon HA-g-PLA/HCDs exposure was quite different. Indeed, the mRNA levels of some genes appeared slightly increased (Fig. 6a). Similar profiles also occurred in irradiated cells. Even if such changes occurred, they did not result in the commitment toward a specific cell death pathway at the used dosage. However, this transcriptional pattern in response to HA-g-PLA/HCDs may be related to the higher potency observed on MCF-7.

A weak effect was observed on the gene expression profile of NIR irradiated MCF-7 cells treated with HA-g-PLA/HCDs. The expression profiles revealed the upregulation of proapoptotic members including CASP7, CASP9, BAX, while the prosurvival genes were downregulated (BCL2) or unchanged (BCL-XL) (Fig. 6a and b). Interestingly, also genes involved in autophagy (BECN1) and necroptosis (TNFα) were upregulated, thus suggesting an overall response to laser treatment, MCF-7-specific, via different mechanisms of viability impairment. This confirms that NIR irradiation of treated MCF-7 cells may result in the reduction of cell viability by triggering concurrent cell death pathways (Nicosia et al., 2020).

As expected, the transcriptional response of MCF-7 upon Doxo exposure with or without irradiation provided a different gene expression pattern. The upregulation of proapoptotic genes (CASP7 and BAX), and the reduction of BCL2 mRNA level, suggested the activation of the apoptotic pathway (Fig. 6a and b) (Minotti et al., 2004; Pilco-Ferreto & Calaf, 2016). As above discussed for MDA-MB-231, the simultaneous upregulation of BECN1 and BCL-XL suggests that an interplay may be responsible for the sustaining of different death pathways (Goodall et al., 2016). This is particularly striking in MCF-7 cell lines showing alteration of apoptotic threshold because of P53 and CASP3 status MCF-7 (Jänicke, 2009; Shi et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2005).

Interestingly, this gene expression signature was boosted upon MCF-7 exposure to HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo and in NIR irradiated HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo treated cells. In addition to BCL2 downregulation, increased mRNA levels of CASP7 and BAX, upregulation was reported also for TNFα and RIPK1 (Fig. 6a and b). Because the necroptotic pathway is regulated via TNFα and RIPK1, which serves as an alternative route of cell death for the overcoming of apoptosis resistance, it is reasonable to hypothesize the concurrent activation of different death mechanisms reinforcing the antitumor effect (Q. Chen et al., 2018; Gong et al., 2019; Jouan-Lanhouet et al., 2012; Lin et al., 1999; Molnár et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake study of HA-g-PLA-HCDs on MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of HA-g-PLA-HCDs to efficiently enter cancer cells overexpressing CD44 receptors and act as contrast agent in fluorescence imaging (FLI) was investigated on both MDA-MB-231 and MFC-7 cell lines. Fluorescence microscopy (λ Ex = 559) was employed to track the nanosystem in living breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7) after 4 h and 24 h of incubation with HA-g-PLA-HCDs at a concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 (Fig. 7 a-b). The HA-g-PLA/HCDs proved capable of entering both cancer cell lines after 4 h and showed excellent contrast in FLI once internalized in both cancer cell lines (Fig. 3 a-b). The nanosystems co-localized with DAPI, suggesting that HCDs are released inside cancer cells and can efficiently diffuse inside nuclei and thought cells. This because the higher nuclear pore size is about 10 nm, smaller than the HA-g-PLA/HCDs diameter (d ≈ 300 nm), and it is possible to hypothesize that the intracellular microenvironment allows HA-g-PLA/HCDs disassembling releasing ultrasmall HCDs (d ≈ 4.5 nm) which may cross nuclei through nuclear pores. Besides, the intracellular disassembling of the superstructures explains well the remarkable anticancer effect observed. A similar trend, even if much more evident, is at the root of the results observed after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 7 a’-b’). Besides, relative fluorescence intensities calculated by fluoresce microphs suggest a higher uptake rate for the MDA-MB-231 cell line (Fig. 7d).

With the aim of understading the role of the HA shell in the uptake phenomena observed, the same experiment was performed by displacing HA pendants of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs with free HA. Thus, a solution of free HA was pre-incubated with both cell lines for 30 min before incubating them with the nanosystem at the same concentration above explored. After that, the uptake was evaluated by fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 7 a’-b’). The ability of HA-g-PLA/HCDs to enter both cancer cell lines was abolished by the CD44 receptor displacement with free HA as the red fluorescence intensity was imperceptible after 24 h of incubation (about 1% if compared with untreat cells, Fig. 7d), implying that the uptake of this superstrucutres can occur preferentially by means of this particular receptor. To corroborate this hypothesis, uptake experiments were performed under the same conditions on bronchial ephytelial cells (16-HBE) who do not express CD44 receptors of the membrane (Pandolfi et al., 2019). As expected, cells appeared completely free from red fluorescence up to 24 h from the incubation, confirming that HA-g-PLA/HCDs can enter cells only by CD44-mediated active endocytosis (Fig. 7c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we designed NIR responsive core-shell hybrid nanocomposites, consisting of an amphiphilic hyaluronic acid-PLA derivative, named HA-g-PLA, self-assembled into micelle-like superstructures in which hydrophobic carbon nanodots (HCDs) are enclosed inside the core by hydrophobic interactions. These hyaluronic acid-dressed superstructures, namely HA-g-PLA/HCDs, were designed to selectively recognize cancer cells overexpressing CD44 receptors (highly affine for HA chains) and for efficient loading and on demand local release of high amount Doxo via NIR-light stimuli. Besides, HA-g-PLA/HCDs were conceived to combine Doxo release to hyperthermia (T ~ 43 °C) and fluorescence imaging, thanks to the excellent optical properties of CDs, so as to have in a single theranostic platform a multimodal tool to selectively and efficiently recognize, kill and monitor cancer cells.

The rational surface passivation of CDs with octylamine allowed obtaining hydrophobic CDs, which maintain the red fluorescence and high photothermal conversion of native CDs. Hence, the obtained HCDs were incorporated inside the hydrophobic core of HA-g-PLA superstructures giving rise to hybrid nanosystems endowed with theranostic properties (i.e., high NIR photothermal conversion into heat, red fluorescence quantum yield of 6%, high Doxo loading capabilities and NIR-sensitive drug release). HCDs also impinge on the self-assembling behavior of HA-g-PLA, since they induced the formation of collapsed superstructures of about 300 nm in diameter (about two times smaller), with well-established hydrophobic core and excellent NIR-sensitive drug delivery properties (Doxo loading >9% and NIR-triggered release twice with respect to unexposed samples). While HA dressing, thanks to the high CD44 recognition capability and hydrophilic features, guarantees high stability in water and promotes cancer cell recognition and efficient cancer cell internalization. Indeed, we demonstrated that HA-g-PLA/HCDs may enter breast cancer cells which overexpress CD44 receptors, and thus can act as a Trojan horse to release heat and actives inside cells. Secondly, HA-g-PLA/HCDs can be used to track cancer cells by fluorescence imaging in the red region.

The high biocompatibility of the HA-g-PLA/HCDs was assessed by in vitro MTS assay in combination with gene expression analysis of common regulated cell death (RCD) pathways, displaying that these superstructures do not induce RCD and mitochondrial stress at the tested concentration. Besides, they proved capable of activating RCD upon NIR-light activation using a common 810 nm diode laser for 300 s under mild power density (7 W cm−2). We also demonstrated that combined chemo-photothermal approaches using Doxo-loaded systems (HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo) and NIR- light exposure provide marked RCD activation like necroptosis and very efficient cancer cell kill (cell death >99%).

From all this it follows that HA-g-PLA/HCDs@Doxo have a perfect mixture of stability, optical and delivery properties that make them promising candidate as non-invasive theranostic agents for precise chemo-photothermal eradication of solid tumors, overcoming cancer multidrug resistance.
